OpenAI said it no longer recommends SWE-Bench Pro as a reliable benchmark for evaluating AI coding performance after an internal audit found that about 30% of its tasks contain design flaws that skew results. The company withdrew its previous recommendation, saying the benchmark has become increasingly unreliable as leading AI models rapidly improve.  The audit examined 731 public tasks using AI research agents and independent reviews by five experienced software engineers. OpenAI found defects in 27%–34% of the dataset, including overly strict tests, vague or incomplete task descriptions, insufficient test coverage, and misleading prompts that can reject correct solutions or allow incomplete fixes to pass. The company also noted that many tasks are automatically generated from real software repositories, where issue descriptions, code changes, and unit tests are often misaligned.   $MSFT  $OPEAZZX.P

OpenAI released a set of national security principles outlining how it will govern partnerships with governments and military organizations as it expands work with the U.S. government and allied nations in cybersecurity and biosecurity. Developed with national security expert David Kris, the framework applies to current and future defense and law enforcement partnerships, including the company's existing collaboration with the U.S. Department of Defense. OpenAI said its contracts prohibit the use of its AI for domestic mass surveillance, directing autonomous weapons systems, or making high-risk automated decisions.  The company has recently established Trusted Access cybersecurity partnerships with Australia, Canada, Japan, South Korea, France, Germany, Poland, the Netherlands and the EU's ENISA under its Daybreak cyber defense program.  $MSFT  $OPEAZZX.P

$MSFT CEO: The 2019 OpenAI deal wasn't a hunch - Microsoft had been chasing natural language for a decade. And the real gamble wasn't the $1B; it was concentrating the company's scarce compute on a single outside lab    Nadella warns that if a company merely buys API access to giant frontier models, the model absorbs the company's workflows and data. Over time, the enterprise cedes its unique intellectual property and competitive moat to a few Silicon Valley model creators    Copilot's third form factor, "Scout", is a long-running agent carrying your corporate identity - it monitors, it has a heartbeat, it "dreams", and you can mint more autopilots with their own IDs and sandboxes    Maia 200, Microsoft's own chip (co-designed w/ Microsoft models & OpenAI models), is already running GPT-5.5 in production across multiple data centers powering Copilot - with "total TCO advantage" over GPUs    Built Cobalt - Microsoft $ARMH processor for compute & benchmarking to improve latency & performace for agentic loops    Using old GPUs in their fleet to accelerate their data warehouse - Fabric is seeing 7x+ performance gains b/c GPU acceleration

@howardlindzon The industry will transition from capital-intensive data center construction (1st phase) to the active monetization of their cloud backlogs (2nd phase). Cloud computing growth continues to accelerate, heavily fueled by the rise of enterprise agentic AI workloads.     IMHO the world still has massive digital runways left to capture. While enterprise AI adoption is growing, trillions of  everyday operational workflows have yet to deploy autonomous AI agents    Global data created has grown  > +3x over last 5yrs to 230+ zettabytes. Meanwhile, AI tokens have exploded from near-zero to tens of quadrillions per month, with no signs of slowing down    Companies are further optimizing margins thru aggressive cost rationalization. This is driven by internalizing custom hardware & a strategic pivot away from expensive 3rd-party LLMs    The market has deservingly has discounted growth concerns for $MSFT - currently trades below the market multiple - Intelligent Cloud segment will need to carry the other 2 segments which will only see growth from increase in prices rather than volume. Should Copilot adoption increase than that is simply icing on the cake    $GOOGL & $AMZN deserve a premiuim b/c their highly diversified core ecosystems support growth outside of pure cloud compute - high margin Ad market continues to grow which should provide more earnings stability     Eventually, (5+ years) we will see a price war develop as hyperscalers look to intentionally squeeze out neocloud capacity - the 3rd phase
